fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BRASCH J:

Final Orders were made in this matter yesterday, 25 February 2026. Due to other commitments that day, the Court was unable to deliver Reasons at the time and advised the parties that they would be provided shortly thereafter. These are those Reasons.
The applicants in this matter, Mrs Yuldasheva and Mr Yuldasheva, are the maternal grandparents (“the maternal grandparents”) of the child X (“the child”).
X was born in 2015 and is 10 years old.
X’s mother, Ms Yuldasheva, died in 2025 (“the mother”); her cause of death is not stated on the Death Certificate. The maternal grandparents depose their daughter, the mother, died from the consequences of an eating disorder.
X’s father is not known. No name appears on the child’s birth certificate and the maternal grandparents both deposed that the mother never mentioned the identity of X’s father. I accept their evidence.
Thus, there is no known father, who would ordinarily be a necessary party under r 3.02 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”).

Background

The maternal grandmother was born in 1965. She is self-employed.
The maternal grandfather was born in 1957. He is a farmer.
The maternal grandparents married in 1998 and remain living together.
Their son, Mr B, was born first. X’s mother, Ms Yuldasheva, was born in 1993. She died nine months ago and intestate.
When X was born, she and the mother lived with the maternal grandparents for approximately one year. Thereafter, X lived with her mother. The mother and child regularly engaged with the maternal grandparents and the wider maternal family.
The maternal grandparents have cared for X since her mother’s passing. After an unsuccessful trial of a school for X in the maternal grandparents’ locale, they moved the location of their own residence so X could resume at the same school she attended when living with her mother.
The maternal grandparents are taking the required steps to apply for Letters of Administration in the Supreme Court of New South Wales with respect to the mother’s estate.

Critical Incident List

The Critical Incident List is a list designed to confer major long-term decision-making responsibility for children upon a non-parent when they have neither an appropriate nor available parent to make urgent decisions for them. That is the case here.
On 19 February 2026, the maternal grandparents filed an Initiating Application, with supporting affidavits and other required documents.
They asked the matter be referred to the Critical Incident List because they needed a conferral of parental responsibility upon them so they could make urgent major long-term decisions about X’s health care. Whilst they were able to secure school enrolments for X, they nevertheless needed authority to deal with the school going forward.
The matter was listed for a first return hearing yesterday, 25 February 2026.
Section 67ZBD Orders were issued to the Department of Communities and Justice (NSW) and the New South Wales Police Force, and returnable prior to the 25 February 2026 listing date.

Material

The applicants relied upon the following material:
(a) Initiating Application filed on 19 February 2026;

(b) Affidavit of Mrs Yuldasheva filed on 19 February 2026;

(c) Affidavit of Mr Yuldasheva filed 19 February 2026;

(d) Affidavit - Non-Filing Family Dispute Resolution Certificate of Mr Yuldasheva filed on 19 February 2026;

(e) Affidavit - Non-Filing Family Dispute Resolution Certificate of Mrs Yuldasheva on 19 February 2026; and

(f) Notice of Child Abuse Family Violence or Risk filed 19 February 2026.

As required by r 8.15(3)(e) of the Rules, the annexures to the first and second applicants’ substantive affidavits were marked as Exhibits 1 and 2 respectively.
The s 67ZBD responses from the Department of Communities and Justice (NSW) and the New South Wales Police Force were respectively marked as Exhibits 3 and 4. The Court provided an outline of their contents to the applicants’ legal representative at the start of the hearing and that copies would be provided to them after the hearing.

Legal principles

Section 60CG of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) imposes a statutory imperative to ensure that a parenting order does not expose a person to an unacceptable risk of family violence and empowers the Court to include in the order any safeguards that it considers necessary for the safety of those affected by the order.
Section 65D(1) of the Act provides that this Court may make such parenting orders as it thinks proper (subject to s 65DAB of the Act, which is irrelevant in this matter). A “parenting order” is defined in s 64B of the Act.
Section 60B of the Act sets out the objects of Part VII of the Act, and that is:
The objects of this Part are:
(a) to ensure that the best interests of children are met, including by ensuring their safety; and

(b) to give effect to the Convention on the Rights of the Child done at New York on 20 November 1989.

Section 60CA of the Act provides that the child’s best interests are a paramount consideration in making a parenting order. Section 60CC of the Act addresses those best interests and provides:
(1) Subject to subsection (4), in determining what is in the child’s best interests, the court must:
(a) consider the matters set out in subsection (2); and
(b) if the child is an Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander child-also consider the matters set out in subsection (3).
The provisions in s 60CC(4) do not apply here. As for s 60CC(1)(b) and (3), X is not an Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander child.

Best interests

Subsection 60CC(2)(a) refers to what arrangements would promote the safety of the child and each person who has the care of the child, whether or not that person has parental responsibility.
I have the benefit of a s 67ZBD report from the New South Wales Police Force; Exhibit 4. It indicates each of the maternal grandparents have some historical records, but mainly as victims or witnesses. No child protection concerns are raised in this report.
I also have the benefit of a s 67ZBD report from the Department of Communities and Justice (NSW), which has been marked as Exhibit 3. They have no records of the child, the deceased mother or either of the maternal grandparents.
Both reports indicate neither the maternal grandparents nor the child have come to the attention of these relevant authorities with respect to child safety concerns.
Another thing that says the maternal grandparents are promoting X’s safety is that they have brought this application so they can make major long-term decisions for her, including for X’s health, counselling support and education. That promotes her physical, psychological and educational safety.
Subsection 60CC(2)(b) concerns the views of the child.
Both maternal grandparents have engaged with X about her living arrangements after her mother’s death. They depose to X telling them she is “happy living with Nanny and Pa” and rated living with them as a 10 out of 10.
Initially, they enrolled X in a school near their (now former) home. However, they were alert and attentive to X not settling at that school. Therefore, they relocated their home to a new residence enabling X to be re-enrolled at her old school. That demonstrates a focus on her views and needs. That re-enrolment must also give the child some comfort to be back in a familiar educational environment and with known friends.
Subsections 60CC(2)(c) and (d) go hand in hand; the developmental, psychological, emotional, cultural needs of the child, and the capacity of the adults or the parties in this matter to meet those needs.
Again, the maternal grandparents have brought this application, which is child focused to X’s needs in and of itself. The maternal grandparents depose to their capacities to meet X’s needs – moving house and changing X back to her old school being a prime example.
Both grandparents describe a home environment which is supportive and nurturing for the child. Upon relocating from their home to a new home so X could return to her familiar school, X has been able to choose her own room, decorations and furnishings. That must have given her a sense of comfort and agency.
They have facilitated X making the Roman Catholic Sacrament of Holy Communion and encourage her practice in that faith. I infer the maternal grandparents are also practicing Catholics.
The maternal grandparents have ensured X has access to counselling services and developed strategies so she feels connected to her mother.
Needless to say, the maternal grandparents have raised their own two children, meaning they are not novices at parenting.
Subsection 60CC(2)(e) speaks to the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so.
In this matter, the mother has passed, and the father is unknown.
However, X is not a stranger to the maternal grandparents or her wider family. The maternal grandparents depose that X is involved with the wider maternal family including an uncle and aunt, and grandaunts and uncles.
Subsection 60CC(2)(f) is anything else.
One of the points of the Critical Incident List is to get parties in and out of the Court as quickly and as expeditiously as a child’s best interests allow. On the evidence before me, including the two s 67ZBD reports, I can see no reason to bring this matter back for a second return.
Not only has X lost her mother, but the maternal grandparents have lost their daughter. All must grieve and focus on their new living arrangements and the loss in their lives.
Hence, the maternal grandparents (and derivatively X) do not need to come back to Court on a second return for some unspecific purpose and for no apparent gain.
Accordingly, X’s best interests are better served by finalising the matter today, so everyone has certainty, and they can get on the with the processes of life.
For those reasons, I will make the Orders that I discussed with the maternal grandparents’ solicitor at the start of the hearing.
